Chapter 12 : Intermolecular Forces: Liquids, Solids, and Phase Changes
12.1 Types of Intermolecular Forces.
12.2 An Overview of Physical States and Phase Changes.
12.3 Quantitative Aspects of Phase Changes.
12.4	Properties of the Liquid State.
12.5	The Uniqueness of Water.
12.6	The Solid State: Structure, Properties, and Bonding.
12.7	Advanced Materials.

Attractive Forces
Intramolecular or bonding forces are found within a molecule. The chemical behavior of each phase of matter is the same because the same constituent particle is present in each case.
H 2 O molecules are present whether the substance is in the solid, liquid, or gas phase.
Intermolecular or nonbonding forces are found between molecules. The physical behavior of each phase of matter is different because the strength of these forces differs from state to state.

Intramolecular Forces vs. Intermolecular Forces
Figure 12.1
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide111.xml]

The Nature of Intermolecular Forces
Intermolecular forces arise from the attraction between molecules with partial charges, or between ions and molecules.
Intermolecular forces are relatively weak compared to bonding forces because they involve smaller charges that are farther apart.

Covalent and Van Der Waals Radii
Figure 12.2A
The van der Waals distance is the distance between two nonbonded atoms in adjacent molecules.
The van der Waals radius is one-half the closest distance between the nuclei of two nonbonded atoms. The V D W radius is always larger than the covalent radius.

Periodic Trends In Covalent And Van Der Waals Radii
Like covalent radii (blue quarter-circles and numbers in the figure), van der Waals radii (red quarter-circles and numbers) decrease across a period and increase down a group.
Figure 12.2B
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide112.xml]

Force | Model | Basis of Attraction | Energy (kJ/mol) | Example
Ion-dipole | Picture of A model of ion-dipole forces is shown. The + ion attracts e⁻ from the other atom. | Ion charge– dipole charge | 40–600 | Picture of A model of H2O is shown with an ion-dipole bond between the O and an Na+ atom.
H bond | Picture of A model of H-bonding is shown. A ion with partial − charge is covalently bonded to H, which has a partial + charge, and is in turn H-bonded to another atom with a partial − charge and a lone pair of electrons. | Polar bond to H– dipole charge (high EN of N, O, F) | 10–40 | Picture of Two H2O molecules are shown. An H on the first is H-bonded to the O on the second molecule.
Dipole-dipole | Picture of A dipole-dipole bond is shown. Concentration of negative charge on one atom bonds to a concentration of positive charge on the adjacent side of the other atom. | Dipole charges | 5–25 | The structure has two molecules of I-Cl bonded by dipole charges between them. The Cl of the first molecule is bonded (dots) to I of the second molecule.

They are also called as dispersion (London) bonds.
Table 12.1

Ion-Dipole and Dipole-Dipole Forces
Ion-dipole forces result when an ion and a nearby polar molecule attract each other.
The most important example of an ion-dipole force takes place when an ionic compound dissolves in water.
Dipole-dipole forces are the attractive forces between the positive pole of one polar molecule and the negative pole of another polar molecule. Dipole-dipole forces only exist for polar molecules.
For compounds of similar molar mass, the greater the molecular dipole moment, the greater the dipole-dipole forces.
Dipole-dipole forces are not as strong as ion-dipole forces.

Polar Molecules and Dipole-Dipole Forces
Figure 12.3
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide113.xml]

Dipole Moment and Boiling Point
Figure 12.4

The Hydrogen Bond
Hydrogen bonding is possible for molecules that have a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a small, highly electronegative atom with lone electron pairs , specifically N, O, or F.
An intermolecular hydrogen bond is the attraction between the H atom of one molecule and a lone pair of the N, O, or F atom of another molecule.
Hydrogen bonds are generally stronger than other dipole-dipole forces.
Substances that exhibit hydrogen bonding have exceptionally high boiling points.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide114.xml]

Hydrogen Bonding and Boiling Point
Figure 12.5
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide115.xml]

Sample Problem 12.1: Problem and Plan
Drawing Hydrogen Bonds Between Molecules of a Substance
PROBLEM: Which of these substances exhibits H bonding? Draw examples of the H bonds between two molecules of
each substance that does. (a) C 2 H 6 (b) CH 3 O H (c)
PLAN: If the molecule does not contain N, O, or F, it cannot form H bonds. If it contains any of these atoms covalently
bonded to H, we draw two molecules in the

Sample Problem 12.1: Solution
SOLUTION:
(a) No N, O, or F, so no H bonds can form.
(b) The H covalently bonded to the O in one molecule forms an H bond to the lone pair on the O of an adjacent molecule:
(c) Two of these molecules can form one H bond between an H bonded to N and the O, or they can form two such H bonds:

Dispersion (London) Forces
Dispersion forces or London forces arise when an instantaneous dipole in one particle induces a dipole in another, resulting in an attraction between them.
Dispersion forces exist between all particles , increasing the energy of attraction in all matter. They are the only force existing between nonpolar particles.
Dispersion forces are stronger for more polarizable particles.
In general, larger particles experience stronger dispersion forces than smaller ones.
As molar mass increases, dispersion forces increase in strength, and so do boiling points.
A molecular shape that has more area allows stronger attractions.

Dispersion Forces Among Nonpolar Particles
Figure 12.6
When atoms are far apart they do not influence one other.
When atoms are close together, the instantaneous dipole in one atom induces a dipole in the other.
The process occurs throughout the sample.

Molar Mass and Trends in Boiling Point
Dispersion forces are stronger for larger, more polarizable particles.
Polarizability correlates closely with molar mass for similar particles.
Figure 12.7
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide116.xml]

Molecular Shape, Intermolecular Contact, and Boiling Point
Figure 12.8

Determining the Intermolecular Forces In a Sample
Figure 12.9
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide117.xml]

Sample Problem 12.2: Problem and Plan
Identifying the Types of Intermolecular Forces
PROBLEM: For each substance, identify the key bonding and/or intermolecular force(s), and predict which substance of the pair has the higher boiling point: (a) MgCl 2 or PCl 3 (b) CH 3 NH 2 or CH 3 F (c) CH 3 O H or CH 3 CH 2 O H (d) Hexane (CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 ) or 2,2-dimethylbutane.
PLAN: We examine the formulas and structures for key differences between members of the pair: Are ions present? Are molecules polar or nonpolar? Is N, O, or F bonded to H? Do the molecules have different masses or shapes?

Sample Problem 12.2 – Solution (a) and (b)
SOLUTION:
(a) MgCl 2 consists of
ions held together by ionic bonding
forces ; PCl 3 , with a trigonal pyramidal geometry, consists of polar molecules, so intermolecular dipole-dipole forces are present. The forces in MgCl 2 are stronger, so it should have a higher boiling point.
(b) CH 3 NH 2 and CH 3 F both consist of polar molecules of about the same
molar mass. CH 3 NH 2 has
bonds, so it can form H bonds . CH 3 F
contains a
bond but no
bond, so dipole-dipole forces occur but
not H bonds. Therefore, CH 3 NH 2 should have the higher boiling point.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide118.xml]

Sample Problem 12.2 – Solution (c) and (d)
SOLUTION:
(c) CH 3 O H and CH 3 CH 2 O H molecules both
contain an
bond, so they can form H
bonds . CH 3 CH 2 O H has an additional
group and thus a larger molar mass, which correlates with stronger dispersion forces; therefore, it should have a higher boiling point.
(d) Hexane and 2,2-dimethylbutane are nonpolar molecules of the same molar mass but different molecular shapes . Cylindrical hexane molecules make more intermolecular contact than more compact 2,2-dimethylbutane molecules do, so hexane should have stronger dispersion forces and a higher boiling point.

Phases of Matter
Each physical state of matter is a phase , a physically distinct, homogeneous part of a system.
The properties of each phase are determined by the balance between the potential and kinetic energy of the particles.
The potential energy, in the form of attractive forces , tends to draw particles together.
The kinetic energy associated with movement tends to disperse particles.

A Comparison of Gases, Liquids, and Solids
Gas | Liquid | Solid
Picture of Molecular scene shows gas particles depicted as spheres moving freely and disorganized. | Picture of Molecular scene shows liquid particles depicted as spheres close together and disorganized. | Picture of Molecular scene shows solid particles depicted as spheres close together and organized.
Kinetic energy dominates the potential energy of intermolecular forces.
On average, gas particles are far apart (low density) and move randomly throughout the container.
The large distance between gas particles means that a gas is highly compressible.
Since the gas particles have freedom of motion, a gas fills its container, taking both the volume and shape of the container.
A gas flows easily through another gas. | Intermolecular attractions between particles are strong enough to pull the particles closer together.
Liquid particles touch each other (higher density) and have limited freedom of motion. The particles move randomly around each other.
Liquids resist an applied force and thus compress only slightly.
The particles still have enough kinetic energy to move randomly around each other, so a liquid conforms to the shape of its container, but it has a surface (it does not fill the container). 
A liquid does flow, but much more slowly than a gas. | Intermolecular forces clearly dominate the kinetic energy of the particles.
The particles are fixed in position next to each other in a regular three-dimensional pattern; solids have relatively high density and very low freedom of motion; particles in a solid just jiggle in place.
The particles are so close together that a solid compresses even less than a liquid.
Since the particles have very little freedom of motion, a solid does not take the shape nor volume of its container.
A solid does not flow significantly.
Table 12.2

Types of Phase Changes and Their Enthalpies
Solid to liquid, and vice versa:
As the temperature increases, the particles in a solid gain kinetic energy and move out of their fixed positions in the process of melting, or fusion; the opposite change is called freezing.
Liquid to gas, and vice versa:
As the temperature increases further, the molecules in the liquid phase gain sufficient kinetic energy to separate from each other completely and form a gas in the process of vaporization; the opposite process, changing from a gas to a liquid, is called condensation.
Solid to gas, and vice versa:
Under certain conditions, as the temperature of a solid increases and the particles gain kinetic energy, they move directly to the gas phase in a process called sublimation; the opposite process is called deposition.

Two Familiar Phase Changes
Figure 12.10
Source: (A) Jose Luis Pelaez Inc/Blend Images LLC; (B) dinadesign /Shutterstock

Heats of Vaporization and Fusion for Several Common Substances
Figure 12.11

Phase Changes and Their Enthalpy Changes
Figure 12.12

Quantitative Aspects of Phase Changes
Within a phase, heat flow is accompanied by a change in
temperature, since the average
of the particles changes.
During a phase change , heat flow occurs at constant temperature, as the average distance between particles changes.

A Heating-cooling Curve For The Conversion Of Gaseous Water To Ice
Figure 12.13
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide120.xml]

Sample Problem 12.3: Problem
Finding the Heat of a Phase Change Depicted by Molecular Scenes
PROBLEM: The scenes below represent a phase change of water. Use values for molar heat capacities and heats of phase changes from the text discussion to find the heat (in k J) released or absorbed when 24.8 g of H 2 O undergoes this change.

Sample Problem 12.3: Plan
PLAN: From the molecular scenes, values given in the text discussion, and the given mass (24.8 g) of water, we have to find the heat that accompanies this change. The scenes show a disorderly, condensed phase at 85.0°C changing to separate molecules at 117°C. Thus, the phase change they depict is vaporization, an endothermic process. The values for molar heat capacities and heats of phase changes are per mole, so we first convert the mass (g) of water to amount (mol). There are three stages: (1) heating the liquid from 85.0°C to 100.°C; (2) converting liquid water at 100.°C to gaseous water at 100.°C; and (3) heating the gas from 100.°C to 117°C. We add the values of q for these stages to obtain the total heat.

Sample Problem 12.3: Solution
SOLUTION:
For Stage 1:
For Stage 2:

Liquid-Gas Equilibrium
Figure 12.14
In a closed flask, the system reaches a state of dynamic equilibrium , where molecules are leaving and entering the liquid at the same rate .
The vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by the vapor on the liquid. The pressure increases until equilibrium is reached; at equilibrium the vapor pressure is constant .

Factors Affecting Vapor Pressure
Vapor pressure is affected by two factors―a change in temperature and a change in the gas itself, that is, in the type and/or strength of intermolecular forces.
As temperature increases , the fraction of molecules with enough energy to enter the vapor phase increases , and the vapor pressure increases .
The weaker the intermolecular forces, the more easily particles enter the vapor phase, and the higher the vapor pressure.

The Effect of Temperature On Vapor Pressure
Figure 12.15
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide121.xml]

Vapor Pressure as a Function of Temperature and Intermolecular Forces
Figure 12.16
Vapor pressure increases as temperature increases.
Vapor pressure decreases as the strength of the intermolecular forces increases.

Quantifying the Effect of Temperature on Vapor Pressure
The Clausius-Clapeyron equation relates vapor pressure to temperature.
The two-point form is used when the vapor pressures at two different temperatures are known.
Equation 12.1

Linear Plots of the Relationship Between Vapor Pressure and Temperature
Figure 12.17

Applying the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation
PROBLEM: The vapor pressure of ethanol is 119 torr at
34.9°C. If
of ethanol is 38.56 kJ/mol, calculate the
temperature (in °C) when the vapor pressure is 760 torr.
PLAN: We are given
P ₁, P ₂, and T ₁ and substitute them
into the Clausius-Clapeyron equation to solve for T ₂. The
value of R here is
so we must convert T ₁ to K
to obtain T ₂, and then convert T ₂ back to °C.

Sample Problem 12.4: Solution
SOLUTION: Convert the units of
to J/mol and the
temperature to kelvins. Substituting the values into the Clausius-Clapeyron and solving for T ₂:
T ₁ = 34.9 ℃ + 273.15 = 308.0 K
Solving for T ₂ and converting to ℃:
T ₂ = 351 K = 78℃

Vapor Pressure and Boiling Point
The boiling point of a liquid is the temperature at which the vapor pressure equals the external pressure.
The normal boiling point of a substance is observed at standard atmospheric pressure or 760 torr.
As the external pressure on a liquid increases, the boiling point increases.

Boiling Point and Intermolecular Forces
Since a substance’s vapor pressure is affected by the strength of its intermolecular forces, and since boiling point is related to vapor pressure, a liquid’s boiling point is affected by the strength of the intermolecular forces.
In general, the weaker the intermolecular forces are, the lower the boiling point.

Iodine Subliming
Figure 12.18
Source: Alexandre Dotta /Science Source

Phase Diagram for C O 2
Figure 12.19

Phase Diagram for H 2 O
Figure 12.20

Sample Problem 12.5: Problem
Using a Phase Diagram to Predict Phase Changes
PROBLEM: Use the phase diagram for carbon (shown below) to describe the phase changes that a sample of carbon undergoes during the following: (a) The sample is heated at 10² bar (99 atm) from 1000 K to 5000 K. (b) The sample is then compressed at 5000 K to 10⁶ bar (990,000 atm).

Sample Problem 12.5: Plan
PLAN: We have to describe the phase changes due to heating at constant pressure (10² bar) and then compressing at constant temperature (5000 K).
(a) Using the phase diagram, we find the starting conditions (10² bar and 1000 K), the point labeled a, and draw a horizontal line from there to point b , at the ending conditions (10² bar and 5000 K). We note any phase-transition curves that the line crosses.
(b) Then, from the new starting conditions at point b (10² bar and 5000 K), we draw a vertical line to the ending pressure (10⁶ bar) and note any phase-transition curves that this line crosses.

Sample Problem 12.5: Solution
SOLUTION:
(a) At 10² bar and 1000 K, the sample is in the form of solid graphite. As it is heated, the graphite sublimes and becomes carbon gas (at around 4400 K).
(b) As it is compressed at 5000 K, the gas first condenses to liquid carbon (at around 300 bar), which then solidifies to diamond (at around 3 × 10⁵ bar).

The Molecular Basis Of Surface Tension
Figure 12.21
A surface molecule experiences a net attraction downward . This causes a liquid surface to have the smallest area possible.
An interior molecule is attracted by others on all sides.
Surface tension is the energy required to increase the surface area of a liquid. The stronger the forces between the particles the higher the surface tension.
Surface tension decreases with increasing temperature.

Surface Tension, Viscosity, and Forces Between Particles
Table 12.3

Capillary Action and the Shape Of The Water or Mercury Meniscus In Glass
Capillarity is the rising of a liquid through a narrow space against the pull of gravity.
A. Water displays a concave meniscus.
B. Mercury displays a convex meniscus.
Figure 12.22
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Viscosity
Viscosity is the resistance of a fluid to flow, and it results from intermolecular attractions that impede the movement of molecules around and past each other.
The stronger the intermolecular forces between particles, the higher the viscosity.
Viscosity decreases with increasing temperature.
Viscosity is affected by molecular shape; among liquids with the same types of intermolecular forces, those that consist of longer molecules having higher viscosities.

Viscosity of Water at Several Temperatures
*The units of viscosity are newton-seconds per square meter.
Table 12.4

H-bonding Ability of water
Each H 2 O molecule can form four H bonds to other molecules, resulting in a tetrahedral arrangement.
Figure 12.23

The Hexagonal Structure of Ice
Ice has an open structure due to H-bonding. Ice is therefore less dense than liquid water.
Figure 12.24
Source: (B) ChaoticMind75/iStock/Getty Images

The Unique Macroscopic Behavior of Water that Emerges from its Atomic and Molecular Properties
Figure 12.25

The Solid State
Solids are divided into two categories:
Crystalline solids have well defined shapes due to the orderly arrangement of their particles.
Amorphous solids lack orderly arrangement and have poorly defined shapes.
A crystal is composed of particles packed in an orderly three-dimensional array called the crystal lattice.

The Beauty of Crystalline Solids
Wulfenite.
Barite.
Beryl (emerald).
Quartz (amethyst).
Figure 12.26

The Crystal Lattice and the Unit Cell
The unit cell is the smallest portion of a crystal lattice that yields the crystal if it is repeated in all directions.
The coordination number is the number of nearest neighbors of a particle in a crystal.
Figure 12.27

Simple Cubic Unit Cell
Figure 12.28A

Body-centered Cubic Unit Cell
Figure 12.28B

Face-centered Cubic Unit Cell
Figure 12.28C

Sample Problem 12.6: Problem
Determining the Number of Particles per Unit Cell and the Coordination Number
PROBLEM: For each of the crystalline solids shown, determine the number of atoms (or ions) per unit cell and the coordination number.
(a) Pd
(b) CuI
(c) Mo

Sample Problem 12.6: Plan
PLAN: To determine the number of particles (atoms or ions) in a unit cell, we count the number of particles at the corners, in the faces, and within the body of the cell. The eight corners are shared by eight other cells for a total of 8 corners × ⅛ particle per corner, or 1 particle; particles in the body of a cell are in that cell only; particles in the faces are shared by two cells, 6 faces × ½ particle per face, or 3 particles. We count the number of nearest neighboring particles to obtain the coordination number.

Sample Problem 12.6: Solution
SOLUTION:
(a) Palladium metal adopts a face-centered cubic unit cell:
The coordination number is 12.
(b) Copper
iodide has a unit cell that can be viewed as a face-centered cubic
array of
interpenetrating a face-centered cubic array of
within the body of the unit cell = 4
Each ion is surrounded by four of the oppositely charged ions: coordination number is 4.
(c) Molybdenum metal adopts a body-centered cubic cell:
The coordination number is 8.

Determining Atomic Radius
PROBLEM: Barium is the largest nonradioactive alkaline earth metal. It has a body-centered cubic unit cell and a density of 3.62 g/cm³. What is the atomic radius of barium?
(Volume of a sphere:

Sample Problem 12.7: Plan
PLAN: An atom is spherical, so we can find its radius from its volume. Use the density to find the volume of 1 mol (137.3 g) of Ba. The metal crystallizes in the body-centered cubic structure, so 68% of this volume is occupied by 1 mol of the Ba atoms themselves. Dividing by Avogadro’s number gives the volume of one Ba atom, from which we find the radius.

Types of Crystalline Solids
Atomic solids consist of individual atoms held together only by dispersion forces.
Molecular solids consist of individual molecules held together by various combinations of intermolecular forces.
Ionic solids consist of a regular array of cations and anions.
Metallic solids exhibit an organized crystal structure.
Network Covalent solids consist of atoms covalently bonded together in a three-dimensional network.

Molecular Orbital Band Theory
Molecular orbital (M O) theory offers a model for metallic bonding called band theory.
The atomic orbitals in a large number of metal atoms combine to form molecular orbitals (M Os ). The energies of the M Os are so close that they form a continuum, or band, of M Os .
The lower portion of the band of M Os are filled with valence electrons and is called the valence band.
The empty, higher energy portion of the M Os is called the conduction band.
Electrons can move from the valence band to the conduction band; this explains the properties of metals such as electrical and thermal conductivity, luster, and malleability.

The Band of Molecular Orbitals in Lithium Metal
Figure 12.38

Electrical Conductivity in a Conductor, Semiconductor, and Insulator
The conducting properties of a substance are determined by the energy gap between the valence and conduction bands of M Os .
Figure 12.39

Crystal Structures and Band Representations of Doped Semiconductors
Pure silicon has an energy gap between its valence and conduction bands. Its conductivity is low at room temperature.
Doping silicon with phosphorus adds
additional valence
These enter the
conductance band, bridging the energy gap and increasing conductivity.
Doping silicon with gallium removes electrons from the valence band and introduces positive ions. Si electrons can migrate to the empty orbitals, increasing conductivity.
Figure 12.40

The p-n Junction
Placing a p-type semiconductor adjacent to an n-type creates a p-n junction. Electrons flow freely in the n-to-p direction.
p-n junctions are used in transistors, solar cells, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs).
Figure 12.41

Structures of Two Typical Molecules that Form Liquid Crystal Phases
Figure 12.42

Expanded View of the Atom Arrangements in Some Ceramic Materials

Polymeric Materials
A polymer is an extremely large molecule, or macromolecule, consisting of a covalently linked chain of smaller molecules, called monomers.
The monomer is the repeat unit of the polymer and the polymer may have hundreds or thousands of repeat units.
The molar mass of a polymer depends on the molar mass of the repeat unit and the degree of polymerization ( n ), the number of repeat units in the chain:
The length of the polymer chain is the degree of polymerization time the length of each repeat unit:

Molar Masses of Some Common Polymers
Table 12.8

Shape of a Polymer Molecule
Most polymers adopt a random coil shape due to the random rotation of the repeat unit that results in the polymer chain turning back on itself many times.
The size of the coiled chain is expressed by its radius of
gyration,
the average distance from the center of the mass
of the molecule to the outer edge of the coil.
is determined
by the degree of polymerization and the length of each repeat unit:

Elastomers
Elastomers are polymers that can be stretched and immediately spring back to their  initial shapes when released.

Polar Molecules and Dipole-Dipole Forces - Text Alternative

The liquid shows dipoles close together and disorganized. Positive end of a dipole interacts with the negative end of any of the adjacent dipoles, and the sequence continues.

The Hydrogen Bond - Text Alternative

The five atom sequences are as follows: sequence 1: a fluorine atom carrying three lone pairs of electrons is bonded to hydrogen atom, which is further bonded to another fluorine atom carrying three lone pairs of electrons; sequence 2: a nitrogen atom carrying a lone pair of electrons is bonded to hydrogen atom, which is further bonded to another nitrogen atom carrying a lone pair of electrons; sequence 3: a fluorine atom carrying three lone pairs of electrons is bonded to hydrogen atom, which is further bonded to an oxygen atom carrying two lone pairs of electrons; sequence 4: an oxygen atom carrying two lone pairs of electrons is bonded to hydrogen atom, which is further bonded to a nitrogen atom carrying a lone pair of electrons; sequence 5: a nitrogen atom carrying a lone pair of electrons is bonded to hydrogen atom, which is further bonded to an oxygen atom carrying two lone pairs of electrons.

Determining the Intermolecular Forces In a Sample - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide21.xml]
The steps involved in the tree diagram are as follows: Ions present: Ions only: Ionic Bonding, Section 9.2; Ions plus polar molecules: Ion-dipole forces; Ions not present: Polar molecules: Dipole-dipole forces; Polar molecules with hydrogen bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine: Hydrogen bonding; Nonpolar molecules: Dispersion forces only. Dispersion forces also present whether ions present or not.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide21.xml]

The Effect of Temperature On Vapor Pressure - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide38.xml]
The horizontal axis shows speed, u sub 1, followed by u sub 2. The vertical axis shows fraction of molecules with a given speed. Approximate data from the graph are as follows: At temperature, T sub 1, fraction of molecules with a given speed increases gradually, reaches a peak at speed u sub 1, gradually decreases, and crosses the mark of kinetic energy needed to overcome intermolecular forces in liquid. Area under the curve beyond the kinetic energy mark is labeled as T sub 1. Molecular scene at temperature, T sub 1, shows five molecules above the lattice moving freely and disorganized. At temperature, T sub 2, which is greater than T sub 1, fraction of molecules with a given speed increases gradually, reaches a peak at speed u sub 2, which is lower than peak at speed u sub 1, gradually decreases, and crosses the mark of kinetic energy needed to overcome intermolecular forces in liquid. Area under the curve beyond the kinetic energy mark is labeled as T sub 2. Molecular scene at temperature, T sub 2, shows nine molecules above the lattice moving freely and disorganized. Text reads, These molecules are moving fast enough to vaporize, and more do so at higher T.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide38.xml]

Sample Problem 12.7: Plan - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide72.xml]
Density of Ba in grams per cubic centimeter are converted to volume in cubic centimeters per mole of Ba metal by finding the reciprocal and multiplying by the molar mass in grams per mole. Volume per mole of Ba metal is converted to volume in cubic centimeters per mole of Ba atoms by multiplying by the packing efficiency. This is converted to volume in cubic centimeters per Ba atom by dividing by Avogadro's number. Placing this value in the equation volume equals four thirds times pi times r cubed and solving for r gives the radius of a Ba atom in centimeters.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide72.xml]

Crystalline and Amorphous Silicon Dioxide - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide88.xml]
Top image: A. The structure shows eight corners and six faces of a cube occupied by silicon atoms. In the interior of the cube, there are four silicon atoms bonded to two silicon atoms opposite to each other in the corners at the top, six silicon atoms on the faces, and two silicon atoms opposite to each other in the corners at the bottom opposite to the atoms at the top through oxygen atoms. Each interior silicon atom is bonded to four oxygen atoms. The interior silicon atoms are arranged as follows: two at the top opposite to each other and two at the bottom opposite to each other and opposite to the atoms at the top. The arrangement of interior silicon atoms is alternative to the exterior atoms.
Bottom image: The structure shows eight corners and six faces of a cube occupied by silicon atoms. In the interior of the cube, there are four silicon atoms bonded to two silicon atoms opposite to each other in the corners at the top, six silicon atoms on the faces, and two silicon atoms opposite to each other in the corners at the bottom opposite to the atoms at the top through oxygen atoms. Each interior silicon atom is bonded to four oxygen atoms. The interior silicon atoms are arranged as follows: two at the top opposite to each other and two at the bottom opposite to each other and opposite to the atoms at the top. The arrangement of interior silicon atoms is alternative to the exterior atoms. The cubic structure is distorted on one side. Therefore the positions of the atoms inside the cube are distorted.
